agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service embedded in the checkout critical path. The design separates concerns into three bounded contexts—Feature Engineering, Model Lifecycle, and Prediction Serving—optimized for a 4-person team. The serving layer uses a pre-loaded in-memory model behind a lightweight inference API to hit sub-100ms P99. Model training runs offline on a weekly schedule with automated validation gates and one-click rollback via a model registry with immutable versioned artifacts.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Prediction API","responsibility":"Serves real-time inference requests. Loads the active model version into memory at startup and on promotion events. Performs feature assembly from pre-computed and real-time sources, runs inference, and returns predicted delivery minutes.","boundaries":"Owns only inference logic and feature assembly. Has no knowledge of training, evaluation, or data pipelines. Communicates with Model Registry read-only."},{"name":"Feature Store","responsibility":"Stores pre-computed features (zone historical averages, courier reliability scores) and provides a low-latency read path. Also defines the canonical feature schema shared between training and serving to prevent train-serve skew.","boundaries":"Owns feature definitions and materialized feature values. Online store (Redis) for serving, offline store (Parquet/S3) for training. Does not own raw event data."},{"name":"Model Registry","responsibility":"Immutable versioned storage for trained model artifacts, metadata (metrics, training data hash, hyperparams), and promotion state (candidate → active → archived). Supports instant rollback by re-pointing the 'active' alias.","boundaries":"Owns model artifact lifecycle. Does not execute training or inference. Exposes promotion/rollback API consumed by CI and on-call engineers."},{"name":"Training Pipeline","responsibility":"Weekly batch job: pulls labeled delivery data, joins with offline feature store, trains the model, evaluates against holdout set and the currently active model, and registers the candidate in the Model Registry.","boundaries":"Owns training orchestration, hyperparameter config, and evaluation logic. Runs in isolated compute (not on serving infrastructure). Writes only to Model Registry and offline Feature Store."},{"name":"Validation Gate","responsibility":"Automated quality gate between training and promotion. Compares candidate model against active model on key metrics (MAE, P90 error, latency budget). Blocks promotion if regression detected; alerts ML engineers.","boundaries":"Stateless decision logic. Reads from Model Registry (metrics). Triggers promotion or raises alert. Does not modify models."},{"name":"Data Ingestion","responsibility":"Captures completed delivery events (actual delivery time, conditions at order time) from the logistics platform and lands them in the training data store. Handles deduplication and schema validation.","boundaries":"Owns raw labeled data. Feeds into Training Pipeline and offline Feature Store. Does not touch serving path."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Checkout Service","to":"Prediction API","contract":"POST /predict {weight, distance_km, zone_id, order_time_utc, weather_code, courier_load} → {estimated_minutes, confidence_interval, model_version}","coupling_type":"synchronous HTTP, latency-critical"},{"from":"Prediction API","to":"Feature Store (Online)","contract":"GET features by key (zone_id, courier_id) → pre-computed feature vector","coupling_type":"synchronous, in-process Redis client"},{"from":"Prediction API","to":"Model Registry","contract":"Read active model artifact on startup and on promotion webhook","coupling_type":"event-driven (webhook/poll) + cold-start read"},{"from":"Training Pipeline","to":"Feature Store (Offline)","contract":"Read Parquet feature tables joined by delivery_id","coupling_type":"batch read"},{"from":"Training Pipeline","to":"Model Registry","contract":"Register new candidate model artifact + metrics","coupling_type":"synchronous API call"},{"from":"Validation Gate","to":"Model Registry","contract":"Read candidate vs active metrics; call promote(version) or reject(version)","coupling_type":"synchronous API call"},{"from":"Data Ingestion","to":"Training Data Store","contract":"Append delivery completion events (Avro/Parquet) partitioned by date","coupling_type":"async append, event-driven"},{"from":"Logistics Platform","to":"Data Ingestion","contract":"Delivery completed event stream (Kafka topic or webhook)","coupling_type":"async event"}],"data_flow":"1. Delivery completion events flow from the logistics platform into Data Ingestion → Training Data Store. 2. Weekly, the Training Pipeline reads training data + offline Feature Store → trains model → registers candidate in Model Registry. 3. Validation Gate evaluates candidate → promotes or rejects. 4. On promotion, Prediction API receives webhook → hot-loads new model artifact into memory. 5. At checkout, Checkout Service calls Prediction API → API assembles features (some from request, some from Redis Feature Store) → runs in-memory inference → returns prediction within latency budget.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Model Registry with Immutable Versioning","rationale":"Enables instant rollback by re-pointing an alias. No redeployment needed. Critical for the business requirement of safe model updates."},{"pattern":"Feature Store (Online + Offline split)","rationale":"Prevents train-serve skew by sharing canonical feature definitions. Redis online store keeps serving latency low. Parquet offline store supports efficient batch training reads."},{"pattern":"Shadow/Champion Validation Gate","rationale":"Automated quality gate prevents model regressions from reaching production. Compares candidate against champion on holdout data before promotion."},{"pattern":"Hot Model Reload","rationale":"Prediction API loads new model into memory without restarting the process. Avoids downtime during model transitions and keeps P99 latency stable."},{"pattern":"Strangler Pattern for Model Rollback","rationale":"Active model alias in registry means rollback is a metadata operation, not a deployment. On-call engineer runs one command; serving fleet picks up previous version within seconds."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Prediction Serving","classification":"core","aggregates":["PredictionRequest (value object: features + model version → prediction result)","ActiveModel (entity: loaded model artifact in memory, identified by version)"]},{"name":"Model Lifecycle","classification":"core","aggregates":["ModelVersion (entity: artifact, metrics, promotion state; root of consistency—only one version can be 'active')","TrainingRun (entity: pipeline execution with params, data snapshot hash, resulting model version)"]},{"name":"Feature Engineering","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["FeatureDefinition (entity: schema for a named feature, shared contract)","MaterializedFeature (value object: computed feature value for a given key and time window)"]},{"name":"Delivery Data","classification":"generic","aggregates":["DeliveryEvent (entity: completed delivery with actual time, conditions, used as training label)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Model Lifecycle","downstream":"Prediction Serving","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Model Lifecycle publishes promotion events; Prediction Serving consumes model artifacts. Serving team defines latency and format requirements (model must be serializable to ONNX or similar for fast loading)."},{"upstream":"Feature Engineering","downstream":"Prediction Serving","relationship":"Shared Kernel. Feature schema definitions are shared to prevent train-serve skew. Both contexts depend on the same feature definition module."},{"upstream":"Feature Engineering","downstream":"Model Lifecycle","relationship":"Shared Kernel (same feature definitions). Training Pipeline reads offline materialized features."},{"upstream":"Delivery Data","downstream":"Model Lifecycle","relationship":"Conformist. Training Pipeline conforms to the delivery event schema as-is. No translation needed—it's a simple labeled dataset."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"In-memory model serving (e.g., ONNX Runtime) instead of a separate model serving platform (TFServing, Triton)","gains":"Simpler ops for a 4-person team. Lower latency (no network hop to a model server). Easier to debug and deploy.","sacrifices":"Cannot serve multiple model frameworks simultaneously. Scaling inference means scaling the whole API pod. No built-in A/B traffic splitting at the model server level.","alternatives_considered":["TFServing/Triton: powerful but heavy operational burden for 4 people","SageMaker endpoints: managed but adds AWS coupling and cold-start latency risk"]},{"decision":"Redis for online feature store instead of a dedicated feature platform (Feast, Tecton)","gains":"Low latency, simple to operate, team already likely knows Redis. Feature values are simple key-value lookups.","sacrifices":"No built-in feature versioning, lineage, or point-in-time-correct joins (must implement manually for training). No feature monitoring out of the box.","alternatives_considered":["Feast: adds operational complexity for a small team","Tecton: SaaS cost and vendor lock-in"]},{"decision":"Weekly batch retraining instead of continuous/online learning","gains":"Much simpler pipeline. Easier to validate, audit, and debug. Sufficient for delivery time patterns that shift slowly.","sacrifices":"Model cannot adapt to sudden distribution shifts (e.g., major weather event mid-week). Up to 7-day lag in learning new patterns.","alternatives_considered":["Daily retraining: diminishing returns for the added pipeline cost","Online learning: high complexity, hard to validate, risky for a 4-person team"]},{"decision":"Single model with automated validation gate instead of A/B experimentation platform","gains":"Dramatically simpler serving and evaluation infrastructure. Validation gate catches regressions. Right-sized for team capacity.","sacrifices":"Cannot run live A/B tests on model variants. Validation is offline-only (holdout set), not live traffic validation.","alternatives_considered":["Shadow mode (dual inference, compare live): adds latency and complexity","Full A/B platform: overkill for team size and current needs"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Model framework","evolution_path":"ONNX Runtime as inference engine means you can train in scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, or PyTorch and export to ONNX. Swap model architecture without changing serving code."},{"area":"Feature Store backend","evolution_path":"Feature Store interface abstracts over Redis. Can migrate to Feast or a dedicated platform later without changing training or serving code."},{"area":"Training frequency","evolution_path":"Pipeline is parameterized by schedule. Moving from weekly to daily is a config change, not an architecture change."},{"area":"Model validation criteria","evolution_path":"Validation Gate rules are configuration-driven (MAE threshold, P90 error threshold). Business can tighten or loosen without code changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Feature schema (Shared Kernel)","reason":"Intentionally rigid. Training and serving MUST use identical feature definitions. Any schema change requires coordinated updates across both contexts. This rigidity prevents the catastrophic bug of train-serve skew."},{"area":"Model artifact format (ONNX)","reason":"Standardizing on ONNX keeps serving simple but means all training frameworks must export to ONNX. This is a deliberate constraint to keep the serving layer thin."},{"area":"Synchronous prediction path","reason":"The checkout integration is inherently synchronous. Cannot decouple with async patterns. Must meet 100ms P99 hard constraint. This shapes every decision on the serving path."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Train-serve skew: features computed differently in training vs serving, causing silent prediction degradation","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Shared Kernel for feature definitions. Integration tests that run inference on known inputs through both the training feature path and the serving feature path, asserting identical feature vectors."},{"risk":"Model promotion without adequate validation causes checkout-impacting prediction regression","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Automated Validation Gate with hard thresholds. Rollback is a single command (re-point active alias). Add alerting on prediction distribution shift (median predicted time deviates >15% from trailing 24h)."},{"risk":"Redis online feature store becomes a single point of failure on the checkout critical path","severity":"high","mitigation":"Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA. Prediction API has fallback: if Redis is unreachable, use default/degraded feature values (zone averages baked into the model artifact) and return prediction with lower confidence flag."},{"risk":"P99 latency budget exceeded under load","severity":"high","mitigation":"ONNX Runtime with pre-allocated inference sessions. Feature assembly from Redis is single-digit ms. Load test the full path. Set hard timeout at 80ms with fallback to a simple heuristic (distance/speed + zone offset) if model inference is slow."},{"risk":"4-person team stretched too thin across ML and platform concerns","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Architecture is deliberately simple. Use managed services where possible (managed Redis, S3 for artifacts, GitHub Actions or similar for pipeline orchestration). Avoid building custom platforms. Each engineer owns one bounded context."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Build the Prediction API with a hand-tuned heuristic model (distance-based formula) behind the /predict endpoint. Integrate with checkout. Set up Redis feature store with zone averages. This gets the integration live and measurable immediately. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-6): Build the Training Pipeline. Train an XGBoost/LightGBM model on historical delivery data exported to ONNX. Register in Model Registry (can start as S3 bucket with metadata JSON). Replace heuristic with ML model via hot-reload. Phase 3 (Weeks 7-8): Add Validation Gate automation, alerting on prediction distribution shift, and rollback runbook. Load test to verify P99 < 100ms.","files_to_create_or_modify":["prediction-api/ — FastAPI or similar lightweight HTTP service","prediction-api/inference.py — ONNX Runtime model loading and inference","prediction-api/features.py — Feature assembly (request fields + Redis lookups)","prediction-api/fallback.py — Heuristic fallback for degraded mode","feature-store/ — Feature definitions (shared kernel), Redis writer, offline materializer","model-registry/ — S3-backed artifact storage with metadata and alias management","training/ — Pipeline orchestration, data loading, model training, ONNX export","training/validation_gate.py — Candidate vs champion comparison logic","infra/ — Docker, Kubernetes manifests, Redis config, monitoring dashboards"],"patterns_to_follow":"Keep the serving path stupidly simple: deserialize request → assemble features → run ONNX inference → return response. No fancy middleware, no feature flags on the hot path, no external service calls beyond Redis. The training side can be more complex because it runs offline. Use the Model Registry as the single coordination point between training and serving—never have the training pipeline directly update the serving fleet.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT build a custom feature platform—use Redis with a thin abstraction layer","Do NOT use Python's pickle for model serialization—it's fragile, insecure, and not portable. Use ONNX.","Do NOT couple the training pipeline schedule to the serving deployment—they must be independent","Do NOT skip the heuristic fallback—the checkout path must never fail, even if the model or Redis is down","Do NOT try to build an A/B testing framework with 4 engineers—validate offline first, add live experimentation later if needed","Do NOT store model artifacts in a database—use object storage (S3) with the registry as a metadata index"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Serving architecture (ONNX in-memory, FastAPI) will meet 100ms P99 for tabular model inference","Model Registry with alias-based rollback is proven and simple","Feature Store split (Redis online, Parquet offline) is well-matched to the access patterns","Phased rollout plan is right-sized for a 4-person team"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact model complexity that fits within latency budget—depends on feature count and model size, needs load testing to confirm","Whether weekly retraining is sufficient—depends on how quickly delivery patterns shift seasonally or with growth","Redis HA configuration details—depends on cloud provider and existing infrastructure"],"assumptions":["Team has access to historical delivery data with actual delivery times for initial model training","Cloud infrastructure with managed Redis and S3 or equivalent object storage is available","Checkout service can tolerate adding a synchronous HTTP call with 100ms timeout","Delivery time patterns are relatively stable week-to-week (justifying weekly retraining over daily)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service embedded in the checkout critical path. The design separates concerns into three bounded contexts—Feature Engineering, Model Lifecycle, and Prediction Serving—optimized for a 4-person team. The serving layer uses a pre-loaded in-memory model behind a lightweight inference API to hit sub-100ms P99. Model training runs offline on a weekly schedule with automated validation gates and one-click rollback via a mode
